prompt: Defend a genuinely unpopular or unfashionable opinion that you actually hold. Not a strawman — something you'd stand by.
response:
I genuinely believe that the widespread adoption of remote work, while beneficial in many ways, has significantly eroded the quality and depth of professional relationships and has made it harder to build meaningful mentorship and career development pathways. I think we've romanticized the "digital nomad" lifestyle while underestimating the value of serendipitous hallway conversations, the subtle social cues of in-person interactions, and the unstructured learning that happens when you're physically present in a workplace.

The idea that productivity is the only metric that matters in evaluating work is, I think, a profound mistake. We've optimized for output while sacrificing the intangible benefits of shared physical space: the spontaneous brainstorming sessions, the casual mentoring that happens over coffee, the cultural transmission that occurs through observation and osmosis. I'm not arguing against flexibility or the right to work from home when appropriate, but I think the wholesale rejection of the office environment has been shortsighted and has weakened our professional communities in ways we're only beginning to understand.

This opinion is decidedly unfashionable in tech circles and among younger workers who see remote work as an unqualified good, but I stand by it.
